## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1 | 3 Marks]**
> **Q1. Define Emotional Intelligence. Name any two pioneers who contributed to the development of EI theory.**

**Model Answer:**
Emotional Intelligence (EI) is the ability of an individual to recognize, understand, manage, and effectively use emotions in oneself and others to guide thinking, behavior, and interpersonal relationships. It encompasses self-awareness, self-regulation, motivation, empathy, and social skills.

The two pioneering contributors to EI theory are:
1. **Peter Salovey and John Mayer (1990)** — who coined the term and developed the Four-Branch Ability Model.
2. **Daniel Goleman (1995)** — who popularized the concept and proposed the Five-Component Mixed Model of EI.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2 | 3 Marks]**
> **Q2. List the five components of Daniel Goleman's model of Emotional Intelligence with a brief one-line meaning of each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Self-Awareness** — Recognizing and understanding one's own emotions, strengths, and weaknesses.
2. **Self-Regulation** — Managing and controlling one's emotions and impulses appropriately.
3. **Motivation** — The internal drive to achieve goals for personal satisfaction beyond external rewards.
4. **Empathy** — Understanding and sharing the feelings of others.
5. **Social Skills** — Managing relationships, building networks, and influencing others effectively.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Format)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Dec 2024 | 14 Marks]**

#### **Question A (Choice 1)**

**(a) Explain in detail the Salovey-Mayer Four-Branch Ability Model of Emotional Intelligence. Discuss each branch with a suitable workplace example. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The Salovey-Mayer Four-Branch Ability Model, refined in 1997, is a hierarchical cognitive ability model of Emotional Intelligence. The branches are arranged in increasing order of complexity, with later branches building upon earlier ones.

**Branch 1: Perception of Emotion**
This is the most basic branch. It refers to the ability to detect, identify, and accurately express emotions in oneself and others. Cues include facial expressions, body language, tone of voice, and even art or music.
- *Workplace Example:* A human resources manager notices that an employee's voice trembles during a one-on-one meeting, suggesting underlying anxiety, even though the employee verbally says everything is "fine."

**Branch 2: Using Emotion to Facilitate Thought**
This branch refers to the ability to harness emotional states to improve cognitive processes such as reasoning, decision-making, problem-solving, and creativity.
- *Workplace Example:* A product designer deliberately schedules brainstorming sessions during a positive, upbeat mood to maximize creative idea generation.

**Branch 3: Understanding of Emotions**
This refers to the ability to comprehend emotional language, recognize the causes and consequences of emotions, and understand how emotions transition and combine into complex states (e.g., guilt, pride, jealousy).
- *Workplace Example:* A team leader understands that an employee's anger (Branch 1) is actually rooted in fear of job loss due to a recent company restructuring, and responds with reassurance rather than confrontation.

**Branch 4: Management of Emotion**
This is the most advanced branch. It refers to the ability to regulate emotions in both oneself and others to achieve personal growth and goal accomplishment.
- *Workplace Example:* A senior manager who is naturally an introvert practices specific techniques (deep breathing, structured responses) to remain calm and effective during high-pressure board meetings.

> The model is hierarchical — Branch 1 must be mastered before Branch 2 can be effective, and so on. This makes the model academically rigorous and performance-testable (e.g., via the MSCEIT test).

**(b) Compare and contrast the Salovey-Mayer Ability Model with Goleman's Mixed Model of Emotional Intelligence. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| Dimension | Salovey-Mayer Ability Model | Goleman Mixed Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Year** | Refined 1997 | 1995 |
| **Type** | Pure ability model (cognitive) | Mixed model (ability + personality + motivation) |
| **Number of Components** | 4 branches | 5 components |
| **Focus** | Mental processing of emotions | Workplace competencies and leadership |
| **Measurement** | Performance-based (MSCEIT) | Self-report + 360° feedback (ECI) |
| **Trainability** | Considered relatively stable traits | Highly trainable through coaching |
| **Application** | Academic and research | Corporate leadership and HRD |

**Similarities:**
- Both consider self-awareness as foundational.
- Both view emotion regulation as a key outcome.
- Both treat EI as a form of intelligence that can complement IQ.
- Both have influenced how EI is trained in organizations.

**Differences:**
- Salovey-Mayer is purely cognitive; Goleman includes personality and motivation.
- Goleman's model is more practical for corporate use; Salovey-Mayer's is more academically rigorous.
- Goleman explicitly connects EI to leadership effectiveness and organizational outcomes, while Salovey-Mayer focuses on individual cognitive processing.

> **Conclusion:** Both models have significantly advanced the understanding of Emotional Intelligence. Salovey-Mayer provides scientific rigor, while Goleman provides practical applicability in industrial settings.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2)**

**(a) "Emotional Intelligence is more important than Intelligence Quotient in determining workplace success." Critically evaluate this statement with reference to Goleman's model. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

This statement, popularized by Daniel Goleman (1995), argues that EI plays a more decisive role in workplace success than traditional IQ. A critical evaluation follows.

**Arguments IN FAVOR (Supporting the Statement):**

1. **Leadership Effectiveness:** Goleman's research indicated that for leadership roles, EI accounted for nearly **67%** of the competencies required for superior performance, while technical skills and IQ contributed only **33%**.

2. **Team Performance:** Teams led by high-EI managers demonstrate better collaboration, lower conflict, and higher productivity compared to teams led by high-IQ but low-EI managers.

3. **Stress Management:** High-EI individuals handle workplace stress, criticism, and setbacks more constructively, sustaining long-term performance.

4. **Customer Relations:** In service and sales engineering roles, Empathy and Social Skills (both EI components) directly impact customer satisfaction, which IQ does not.

5. **Conflict Resolution:** EI provides the tools — self-regulation, empathy, communication — required to manage workplace disputes effectively, a skill IQ does not address.

**Arguments AGAINST (Counter-points):**

1. **Role-Specific Relevance:** For highly technical or research roles (e.g., algorithmic design, pure R&D), high IQ and domain expertise may be more critical than EI.

2. **Measurability:** IQ has a long-established, standardized measurement system, while EI assessment tools are still debated for validity and cultural bias.

3. **Not a Replacement:** Goleman himself clarified that EI complements IQ rather than replaces it. A minimum cognitive threshold is required for most jobs.

4. **Subjectivity in Assessment:** Unlike IQ, EI self-report measures are vulnerable to social desirability bias.

**Conclusion:**
For roles involving significant human interaction — leadership, team management, client engagement, negotiation — EI is demonstrably more important than IQ. For purely technical or analytical roles, IQ remains vital. The balanced view is that **both IQ and EI together predict workplace success, with EI often being the differentiating factor** in mid-to-senior career stages.

**(b) Discuss the role of Emotional Intelligence in the following industrial psychology applications: (i) Recruitment and Selection, (ii) Leadership Development, (iii) Stress Management. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Recruitment and Selection:**
Modern organizations increasingly incorporate EI assessment into their hiring processes, especially for leadership, customer-facing, and team-based roles. Tools like the **EQ-i 2.0**, **MSCEIT**, and **Goleman's ECI** are used alongside technical interviews. High-EI candidates tend to communicate better, handle interview pressure more effectively, and demonstrate higher cultural fit. Companies such as **L'Oréal, PepsiCo, and the U.S. Air Force** have reported significant improvements in selection validity after incorporating EI testing.

**(ii) Leadership Development:**
EI is now considered the cornerstone of leadership development programs. Goleman's research showed that **EI competencies are twice as important as IQ and technical skills combined** in distinguishing outstanding leaders. Companies like **Google, IBM, and Tata** invest heavily in EI-based leadership training, focusing on the five components: self-awareness, self-regulation, motivation, empathy, and social skills. High-EI leaders inspire trust, communicate vision effectively, and retain talent.

**(iii) Stress Management:**
High EI equips individuals to perceive stress accurately (Branch 1), use emotional awareness to cope (Branch 2), understand the source of stress (Branch 3), and manage stress responses (Branch 4). In high-pressure industries such as IT, BPO, manufacturing, and emergency services, EI-based interventions — mindfulness training, cognitive reframing, peer support programs — reduce absenteeism, burnout, and turnover.

> **Conclusion:** EI is no longer a "soft skill" but a strategic industrial psychology tool that drives hiring accuracy, leadership pipeline strength, and employee well-being.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting Author Names and Years:** Always cite "Salovey and Mayer (1990/1997)" and "Goleman (1995)" — failing to do so costs **1 to 2 marks** per question.
> 2. **Confusing the Models:** Many students mix up the 4 branches of Salovey-Mayer with the 5 components of Goleman. Memorize them as separate entities.
> 3. **No Workplace Examples:** For 14-mark questions, examiners expect **at least 2 workplace examples** per model. A purely theoretical answer with no industrial application will be capped at **60–70% of the marks**.
> 4. **Treating EI as "Soft Skill":** EI is a measurable, evidence-based psychological construct. Referring to it as "just being nice" is a major error.
> 5. **Ignoring the "Why":** Do not just list components — explain *why* each is important and *how* it applies in industrial settings.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These Key Points:**

- **Definition:** EI = the ability to recognize, understand, use, and manage emotions (in self and others).
- **Term Coined By:** **Salovey and Mayer, 1990**.
- **Popularized By:** **Daniel Goleman, 1995** (book: *Emotional Intelligence: Why It Can Matter More Than IQ*).
- **Salovey-Mayer Model = 4 Branches:** Perception → Using → Understanding → Managing (hierarchical, cognitive).
- **Goleman Model = 5 Components:** Self-Awareness, Self-Regulation, Motivation, Empathy, Social Skills.
- **Bar-On Model = 5 Scales, 15 Sub-scales** (EQ-i 2.0 assessment tool).
- **Measurement Tools:** MSCEIT (Salovey-Mayer), ECI (Goleman), EQ-i 2.0 (Bar-On).
- **Goleman's Claim:** EI accounts for up to **67%** of competencies distinguishing outstanding leaders.
- **Workplace Application Areas:** Recruitment, leadership development, team building, conflict resolution, stress management, customer relations.
- **Industrial Psychology Link:** EI predicts job performance, leadership success, job satisfaction, and reduced turnover.
- **Trainability:** Goleman says EI is highly trainable; Salovey-Mayer view it as more stable but still improvable.
- **Historical Roots:** Thorndike's "Social Intelligence" (1920) and Gardner's "Multiple Intelligences" (1983).
- **For 3-mark questions:** Define EI + cite 1 model + 1 example.
- **For 14-mark questions:** Define → Explain model with examples → Compare models → Apply to industrial settings → Conclude.
- **Always cite:** Year and author when mentioning a model — examiners reward this.
- **Common Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse "Emotional Intelligence" with "Emotional Personality" or "Empathy alone." EI is a multidimensional construct.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Employee Selection: Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT742 - INDUSTRIAL PSYCHOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Employee Selection: Techniques ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Employee Selection** is the systematic process of choosing the most suitable candidate from a pool of applicants who have successfully cleared the preliminary screening phase. In the context of Industrial Psychology (KTU PESFT742), selection is operationalized as the **predictive decision-making procedure** where organizations utilize validated psychological instruments, behavioral assessments, and structured interviews to forecast a candidate's future job performance, organizational fit, and tenure stability. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** > *"Selection is the process of differentiating between applicants in order to identify (and hire) those with a greater likelihood of success in a job."* > — Derived from the standard Industrial/Organizational Psychology framework adopted by KTU PESFT742 Module 3. ### 1.2 Selection vs. Recruitment: A Critical Distinction | Attribute | Recruitment | Selection | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Objective** | Attract a large pool of applicants | Reject unsuitable; select the best | | **Approach** | Positive (Adds to pool) | Negative (Eliminates from pool) | | **Outcome** | Group of job-seekers | Single (or few) hired candidate(s) | | **Psychological Nature** | Stimulus-generation behavior | Predictive evaluation behavior | > [!NOTE] > **Recruitment is a Positive Process** (building a candidate pool), whereas **Selection is a Subtractive/Negative Process** (systematic rejection leading to a single hire). This distinction is a guaranteed 3-mark question in KTU ESE. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Medical Diagnosis" of Hiring Imagine a cardiologist diagnosing a patient. The doctor does not run a *single* test — they run an **ECG**, a **blood test**, a **stress test**, and a **physical examination**. Each test eliminates one possibility and confirms another. **Employee Selection works identically:** - The **Application Blank** is the preliminary health check-up. - The **Aptitude Test** is the ECG (measures underlying capacity). - The **Personality Test** is the blood test (measures internal traits). - The **Interview** is the doctor's final consultation. - The **Reference Check** is the previous medical history. Just as the doctor must use *multiple diagnostic tools* to reach a reliable diagnosis, an HR psychologist must use **multiple selection techniques** to reach a reliable hiring decision — this is called the **Multiple-Hurdle Model** or **Compensatory/Combined Approach**. ### 1.4 Core Definitions for KTU Board Examination > [!IMPORTANT] > **Selection Ratio (SR)** — One of the most important metrics in selection. Defined as: > $$\text{Selection Ratio} = \frac{\text{Number of Candidates Selected}}{\text{Number of Candidates Applied}}$$ > A *lower* selection ratio indicates a *more selective* (and statistically reliable) process. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Selection Process: Sequential Hurdle Model The KTU 2024 scheme classifies selection techniques along a **predictive validity continuum**. Each technique has a different cost, reliability, and predictive power. The standard KTU classification is as follows: #### **Step 1: Preliminary Screening (Application Blank / Resume Screening)** - **Why:** Filters out the obviously unqualified at near-zero cost. - **How:** Uses biodata, education, experience match. - **Validity:** Low (r ≈ 0.10–0.15) but highly cost-efficient. #### **Step 2: Selection Tests (Psychometric Battery)** This is the *heart* of industrial psychology selection. It includes: **(a) Aptitude Tests** — Measure *capacity* to learn a job. - *Mental Ability / Intelligence Tests* (e.g., Wonderlic Personnel Test) - *Mechanical Aptitude Tests* - *Psychomotor / Clerical Aptitude Tests* - *Numerical & Verbal Reasoning Tests* **(b) Personality Tests** — Measure *disposition* and *behavioral tendencies*. - *16 PF (Cattell)*, *Big Five (OCEAN)*, *Myers-Briggs Type Indicator (MBTI)* - **Projective Tests:** Rorschach Inkblot, Thematic Apperception Test (TAT) **(c) Interest Inventories** — Measure *career orientation* (e.g., Strong Interest Inventory, Kuder Occupational Interest Survey). **(d) Achievement Tests** — Measure *what the candidate already knows* (e.g., typing speed, coding test). **(e) Situational Judgment Tests (SJT)** — Present hypothetical job scenarios and assess decision-making. #### **Step 3: Selection Interview** - **Structured Interview** — Pre-defined questions, scoring rubric (Validity r ≈ 0.51 — highest among interviews). - **Unstructured Interview** — Conversational, low validity (r ≈ 0.20). - **Behavioral Interview (STAR Method)** — Past behavior as predictor of future behavior. - **Panel Interview** — Multiple interviewers. - **Stress Interview** — Tests response under pressure. #### **Step 4: Assessment Centre** - A multi-day, multi-exercise simulation (in-basket, role-play, group discussion). - Validity r ≈ 0.36–0.45; highest for managerial selection. #### **Step 5: Background & Reference Verification** - Education verification, employment history, criminal record. #### **Step 6: Medical / Physical Examination** - Ensures physical fitness for job demands (especially in manufacturing, defense). #### **Step 7: Final Selection & Placement Decision** --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Metrics for KTU PESFT742 Module 3 Examinations** | Metric / Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Range / Interpretation | Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Selection Ratio (SR)** | $SR = \dfrac{\text{Selected}}{\text{Applied}}$ | $0 \le SR \le 1$ (Lower = more selective) | Dimensionless | | **Base Rate (BR)** | Proportion of applicants who would be successful if randomly selected | E.g., $0.50$ (50% success) | Dimensionless | | **Validity Coefficient (r)** | Pearson correlation between test score & job performance | $0$ to $+1$ (Higher = better predictor) | Correlation (r) | | **Utility (Taylor-Russell)** | $\Delta U = N \cdot SR \cdot (BR_u - BR_s)$ | Measures gain from using a selection test | Performance Units | | **Reliability Index** | $r_{xx} = \dfrac{n \cdot r_{xy}}{1 + (n-1) r_{xy}}$ (Spearman-Brown) | Used when combining multiple tests | Correlation | | **Multiple Cutoff Score** | Boolean conjunction of multiple thresholds | Improves selection accuracy | Boolean | | **Compensatory Score** | $S_{total} = w_1 T_1 + w_2 T_2 + \ldots + w_n T_n$ | Weighted sum of test scores | Composite Score | | **Yield Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Number at step } k}{\text{Number at step } k-1}$ | Tracks pipeline efficiency | Ratio | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Questions on the **Taylor-Russell Utility Model** and **Selection Ratio's impact on validity** are extremely high-yield. Memorize the inverse relationship: *Lower Selection Ratio → Higher Validity of the selection program.* --- ### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Industrial Settings 1. **IT/Software Industry** — Cognitive aptitude tests (HackerRank, Codility) + Cultural-fit interview + Personality inventory (Hogan Assessments). 2. **Aviation & Defense** — Psychomotor tests, stress interviews, multi-day Assessment Centres. 3. **BPO / Customer Service** — Voice & accent tests + SJTs + Emotional Intelligence (EQ) tests. 4. **Manufacturing** — Mechanical aptitude + Manual dexterity tests + Physical fitness exam. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Selection Ratio and Validity Relationship The relationship between Selection Ratio (SR) and the **expected validity gain** of a selection program can be derived from the **Taylor-Russell Tables** (1940), a foundational Industrial Psychology tool. **Step 1 — Define the Base Rate (BR)** Let $BR$ be the proportion of current employees who would be considered "successful" without any selection test. For a random hire: $$\text{Success Probability} = BR$$ **Step 2 — Introduce a Validated Selection Test** Let $r_{xy}$ be the validity coefficient between test score ($x$) and job performance ($y$). **Step 3 — Calculate the Expected Success Rate with the Test ($BR_s$)** Using the bivariate normal distribution assumption, the success rate after selection is: $$BR_s = \int_{z_{cut}}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2} \, dz$$ where $z_{cut}$ is the standardized cutoff score determined by the Selection Ratio. **Step 4 — Taylor-Russell Utility Approximation** For a small selection ratio ($SR < 0.20$), the approximate gain is linear: $$\Delta U = N \cdot SR \cdot (BR_s - BR_u)$$ where: - $N$ = total number of applicants - $BR_u$ = base rate *without* selection (random hiring) - $BR_s$ = base rate *with* selection **Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example** *Suppose a firm receives 100 applications (N = 100), the base success rate is 0.30 (BR = 0.30), and the selection ratio is 0.20 (i.e., 20 candidates will be hired). After administering a test with validity r = 0.50, the Taylor-Russell table predicts $BR_s \approx 0.58$.* **Calculation:** $$\Delta U = 100 \times 0.20 \times (0.58 - 0.30)$$ $$\Delta U = 20 \times 0.28 = 5.6 \text{ additional successful hires}$$ This means out of 20 hires, approximately **5.6 additional employees** will succeed compared to random hiring — a clear monetary and operational gain. --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Validity of Selection Techniques | Selection Technique | Mean Validity (r) | Cost | Time Required | KTU Exam Weightage | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | **Structured Interview** | **0.51** | Medium | 30–60 min | High | | **Cognitive Ability Test** | **0.51** | Low | 30–90 min | High | | **Assessment Centre** | **0.36** | Very High | 1–3 days | Medium | | **Unstructured Interview** | 0.20 | Medium | 30 min | Medium | | **Integrity / Personality Test** | 0.31 | Low | 20–45 min | High | | **Work Sample Test** | **0.54** | High | Hours | Medium | | **Reference Check** | 0.18 | Low | Days | Low | | **Application Blank / Biodata** | 0.15 | Very Low | Minutes | Medium | | **Interest Inventory** | 0.10–0.15 | Low | 30 min | Low | > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Insight:** The *Work Sample Test* (r ≈ 0.54) and *Structured Interview* (r ≈ 0.51) have the **highest predictive validities**. This is a frequent KTU question: *"Which selection technique has the highest validity?"* --- ### 3.3 Analytical Step: The Multiple Cut-Off vs. Compensatory Decision Rule **Step A — Multiple Cut-Off (Conjunctive Rule)** A candidate must pass every test at a minimum threshold: $$S_{final} = \mathbb{1}[T_1 \ge c_1] \cdot \mathbb{1}[T_2 \ge c_2] \cdot \ldots \cdot \mathbb{1}[T_n \ge c_n]$$ where $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function (1 if true, 0 if false). **Step B — Compensatory Rule** Weakness in one test can be offset by strength in another (weighted sum): $$S_{final} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot T_i$$ where $\sum w_i = 1$. **Step C — Industry Best Practice (Hybrid)** Most KTU-recommended Industrial Psychology models use a **Compensatory Weighted Score for cognitive + aptitude tests**, followed by a **Conjunctive Multiple Cut-Off** for personality/ethical tests (zero tolerance for integrity violations). --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Sequential Hurdle Model of Employee Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Application Received"] --> nodeB["Step 1: Preliminary Screening"] nodeB --> nodeC{"Meets Minimum
Qualifications?"} nodeC -- "NO" --> nodeD["Reject Candidate"] nodeC -- "YES" --> nodeE["Step 2: Selection Tests"] nodeE --> nodeF["Aptitude Test"] nodeF --> nodeG["Personality Inventory"] nodeG --> nodeH["Interest Inventory"] nodeH --> nodeI{"Combined Score
Passes Cutoff?"} nodeI -- "NO" --> nodeD nodeI -- "YES" --> nodeJ["Step 3: Selection Interview"] nodeJ --> nodeK["Structured Interview"] nodeK --> nodeL["Behavioural STAR"] nodeL --> nodeM{"Interview Score
Acceptable?"} nodeM -- "NO" --> nodeD nodeM -- "YES" --> nodeN["Step 4: Assessment Centre"] nodeN --> nodeO["Step 5: Reference Check"] nodeO --> nodeP["Step 6: Medical Exam"] nodeP --> nodeQ{"Final Selection
Decision"} nodeQ -- "SELECTED" --> nodeR["Job Offer and
Placement"] nodeQ -- "REJECTED" --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeS["Rejection Letter
and Feedback"] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Architecture of an Integrated Selection Battery ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph inputBlock["INPUT STAGE"] i1["Application Blank"] i2["Biodata Scoring"] end subgraph psychometricBlock["PSYCHOMETRIC BATTERY"] p1["Cognitive Aptitude"] p2["Personality Test"] p3["Interest Inventory"] p4["Situational Judgement"] end subgraph interviewBlock["INTERVIEW STAGE"] iv1["Structured Interview"] iv2["Behavioural Interview"] end subgraph assessmentBlock["ASSESSMENT CENTRE"] ac1["In-Basket Exercise"] ac2["Group Discussion"] ac3["Role Play Simulation"] end subgraph decisionBlock["DECISION ENGINE"] d1["Weighted Composite Score"] d2["Multiple Cut-Off Check"] d3["Final Selection Ratio"] end subgraph outputBlock["OUTPUT"] o1["Selected Candidate"] o2["Rejected Candidate Pool"] end i1 --> i2 --> p1 p1 --> p2 --> p3 --> p4 p4 --> iv1 --> iv2 iv2 --> ac1 ac1 --> ac2 --> ac3 ac3 --> d1 d1 --> d2 --> d3 d3 --> o1 d3 --> o2 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map: Relationship Between Selection Techniques ```mermaid flowchart TD root["Employee Selection Techniques"] root --> A["Tests"] root --> B["Interviews"] root --> C["Assessment Centres"] root --> D["Background Checks"] A --> A1["Aptitude Cognitive"] A --> A2["Personality Projective"] A --> A3["Interest Inventories"] A --> A4["Achievement Tests"] A --> A5["Situational Judgement"] B --> B1["Structured Highest Validity"] B --> B2["Unstructured Low Validity"] B --> B3["Panel Interview"] B --> B4["Stress Interview"] C --> C1["In-Basket"] C --> C2["Group Discussion"] C --> C3["Role Play"] D --> D1["Reference Verification"] D --> D2["Education Verification"] D --> D3["Medical Examination"] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Question 1.** *(Conceptual Definition — 3 Marks)* **Define Employee Selection. How is it different from Recruitment?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]` **Model Answer:** Employee Selection is the systematic process of evaluating and choosing the most suitable candidate from a pool of applicants who have been recruited, with the objective of predicting future job performance and organizational fit using validated psychological instruments and assessment procedures. **Key Differences:** - **Recruitment** is a *positive, expansionary* process aimed at generating a large pool of job applicants (e.g., advertising, campus hiring). It is a **pre-hiring** activity. - **Selection** is a *negative, subtractive* process aimed at eliminating unsuitable candidates and hiring the best fit (e.g., tests, interviews). It is a **hiring** activity. In essence, **Recruitment brings candidates in; Selection filters them out** — making it a more psychologically rigorous, validity-driven process. --- **Question 2.** *(Recall — 3 Marks)* **List and briefly explain any THREE selection techniques used in industrial settings.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **Model Answer:** 1. **Aptitude Tests (e.g., Cognitive Ability Tests):** These measure a candidate's *capacity to learn* the job rather than existing knowledge. High predictive validity (r ≈ 0.51). 2. **Structured Interviews:** Pre-defined questions with standardized scoring rubrics. Currently the highest-validity interview format (r ≈ 0.51). Behavioral questions follow the STAR (Situation-Task-Action-Result) framework. 3. **Assessment Centres:** Multi-day, multi-exercise evaluation (in-basket, role-play, group discussion). Particularly effective for managerial selection (r ≈ 0.36–0.45). 4. *(Optional third)* **Personality Tests** — Measure traits like conscientiousness, emotional stability; validity r ≈ 0.31. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ## **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model | KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3]` ### (a) Explain in detail the various Selection Techniques used in Industries. Discuss their relative validity and applicability. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** Selection techniques form the operational backbone of industrial hiring. They are classified by their **predictive validity** (correlation with job performance), **cost**, and **applicability**. **1. Application Blank / Biodata Screening** - The first hurdle; filters based on education, experience, and skills. - Low validity (r ≈ 0.15) but extremely cost-efficient. - Best suited for **high-volume entry-level hiring** (e.g., BPO). **2. Aptitude Tests** - Measure *ability*: intelligence, numerical, verbal, mechanical, clerical. - High validity (r ≈ 0.51). Validated against job performance via criterion-related validation studies. - Best for **technical/engineering roles** where cognitive capacity is critical. **3. Personality Inventories** - Measure *disposition*: Big Five (OCEAN), 16PF, MBTI. - Validity r ≈ 0.31. Strongly predictive for traits like conscientiousness. - Used for **managerial and customer-facing roles**. **4. Selection Interviews** - **Structured** (r ≈ 0.51) — pre-set questions, scoring rubric. - **Unstructured** (r ≈ 0.20) — conversational; least reliable. - **Behavioral (STAR)** — focuses on past behavior. **5. Assessment Centres** - Multi-day simulations (in-basket, role-play, group discussion). - Validity r ≈ 0.36–0.45. Highest for **managerial and leadership roles**. **6. Work Sample Tests** - Direct simulation of job tasks. - Highest validity (r ≈ 0.54). E.g., a coding test for a developer. **7. Reference & Background Checks** - Verify integrity, prior performance. - Validity r ≈ 0.18. **8. Medical Examination** - Ensures physical fitness for job demands (e.g., aviation, defense). **Relative Validity Ranking (Highest to Lowest):** 1. Work Sample Test (0.54) 2. Structured Interview / Cognitive Test (0.51) 3. Assessment Centre (0.36–0.45) 4. Personality Test (0.31) 5. Unstructured Interview (0.20) 6. Reference Check (0.18) 7. Biodata (0.15) **Conclusion:** No single technique is sufficient. Best practice combines **cognitive tests + structured interview + personality inventory** for maximum predictive accuracy. --- ### (b) What is the Selection Ratio? Explain its impact on the validity of a selection program with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** The **Selection Ratio (SR)** is the proportion of applicants who are eventually selected, defined as: $$SR = \frac{\text{Number of Candidates Selected}}{\text{Number of Candidates Applied}}$$ **Range:** $0 \le SR \le 1$. - $SR = 1$ → Everyone is hired (no selection). - $SR \to 0$ → Highly selective (e.g., top-IIT admissions). **Impact on Validity:** According to the **Taylor-Russell Tables**, there is an **inverse relationship** between Selection Ratio and the validity of a selection program: - **Lower SR → Higher reliability and validity** of selection decisions. - **Higher SR → Diminishing validity**, approaching random selection. This is because when the selection ratio is low, only the top-scoring candidates (who are genuinely most likely to succeed) are chosen, making the program's predictive power statistically stronger. **Worked Example:** *Case 1 — IT Company Hiring:* 1,000 applications received; 10 hired. $$SR = \frac{10}{1000} = 0.01$$ This is highly selective; validity of selection program is very high. *Case 2 — Local Retail Store:* 50 applications; 45 hired. $$SR = \frac{45}{50} = 0.90$$ Barely selective; even a valid test offers little incremental value here. **Practical Implication:** Organizations should aim to **inflate the applicant pool** (through recruitment) so that the Selection Ratio is *kept low* (typically $SR < 0.20$ for high-stakes roles). This strategy maximizes the **Utility of Selection** as per the Taylor-Russell model. --- ## **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model | KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3]` ### (a) Discuss the different types of Psychological Tests used in Employee Selection. Explain the difference between Aptitude and Personality tests with examples. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Classification of Psychological Tests:** **1. Aptitude Tests (Ability Tests)** — Measure *capacity*. - *Intelligence Tests:* Wonderlic, Raven's Progressive Matrices. - *Mechanical Aptitude:* Used for technicians, engineers. - *Clerical Aptitude:* Speed and accuracy in clerical tasks. - *Numerical & Verbal Reasoning:* Used in IT, banking. **2. Personality Tests** — Measure *disposition and traits*. - *Self-Report Inventories:* 16PF, Big Five (OCEAN), MBTI, MMPI. - *Projective Tests:* Rorschach Inkblot, Thematic Apperception Test (TAT). **3. Interest Inventories** — Measure *career preference*. - Strong Interest Inventory, Kuder Occupational Interest Survey. **4. Achievement Tests** — Measure *existing knowledge*. - Typing tests, domain-specific certification tests. **5. Situational Judgement Tests (SJT)** — Hypothetical scenarios. **Comparison: Aptitude vs. Personality Tests** | Attribute | Aptitude Test | Personality Test | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **What it measures** | Maximum capacity / ability | Behavioral tendencies / traits | | **Nature** | Cognitive, performance-based | Affective, self-reported | | **Format** | Multiple-choice, timed tasks | Likert-scale questionnaires | | **Validity (r)** | 0.51 (high) | 0.31 (moderate) | | **Example Test** | Wonderlic Personnel Test | Big Five Inventory (BFI) | | **Best Use Case** | Engineer, analyst, programmer | Manager, salesperson, counselor | | **Faking Resistance** | High (right/wrong answers) | Moderate (candidates may fake) | **Conclusion:** Both are complementary. Aptitude tests predict "can the person *do* the job?" while Personality tests predict "will the person *enjoy and sustain* the job?" --- ### (b) Explain Assessment Centres as a selection technique. What are their key components and why are they considered highly effective for managerial selection? **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** An **Assessment Centre** is a multi-day, standardized evaluation process where multiple candidates are assessed by multiple trained assessors using multiple simulation exercises. It is one of the most sophisticated methods of behavioral observation in Industrial Psychology. **Key Components / Exercises:** 1. **In-Basket Exercise** — Candidate handles a stack of memos, emails, and tasks simulating a manager's inbox. Tests **prioritization, delegation, and decision-making**. 2. **Group Discussion** — Candidates discuss a business problem. Tests **leadership, persuasion, and interpersonal skills**. 3. **Role Play** — Simulated interaction with a "subordinate," "client," or "angry customer." Tests **conflict resolution and communication**. 4. **Case Study / Presentation** — Candidates analyze a business case and present recommendations. Tests **analytical and communication skills**. 5. **Behavioral Interview (within the Centre)** — Structured probing of past experiences. **Why Effective for Managerial Selection:** - **High Validity (r ≈ 0.36–0.45)** — Multiple exercises triangulate performance prediction. - **Multiple Trained Assessors** — Reduces individual assessor bias. - **Behavioural Simulation** — Past behavior under simulated pressure predicts future behavior. - **Multiple Dimensions Assessed** — Leadership, communication, analytical thinking, teamwork, initiative, etc. - **Real-World Relevance** — Mimics actual managerial challenges. **Limitations:** - **Very expensive** (₹15,000–₹50,000+ per candidate). - **Time-intensive** (1–3 days). - Requires **expert assessors** and standardized protocols. **Conclusion:** Despite high costs, Assessment Centres remain the **gold standard for senior/managerial selection** because their predictive validity and behavioral realism outweigh the financial investment for high-impact roles. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse Selection with Recruitment.** Selection is *subtractive*, Recruitment is *additive*. Many students lose 2 marks here. > 2. **Always cite the validity coefficient (r)** when discussing a selection technique's reliability — this is a frequent 2-mark sub-question. > 3. **Selection Ratio formula:** $SR = \dfrac{\text{Selected}}{\text{Applied}}$ — NOT $\dfrac{\text{Rejected}}{\text{Applied}}$. > 4. **Structured Interview** has higher validity than Unstructured Interview — this is the most commonly reversed fact. > 5. **For 14-mark answers, always structure with headings and tables** — KTU examiners reward visual organization. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PESFT742 Module 3** - [ ] **Employee Selection** = *subtractive* process of choosing the best-fit candidate using validated psychological instruments. - [ ] **Recruitment ≠ Selection** — Recruitment attracts; Selection filters. - [ ] **Selection Ratio (SR)** = Selected ÷ Applied. **Lower SR = Higher Validity.** - [ ] **Taylor-Russell Tables** — Predict selection success rate given BR, SR, and r. - [ ] **Selection Techniques Hierarchy (by validity, highest → lowest):** - Work Sample Test (0.54) - Structured Interview (0.51) - Cognitive Ability Test (0.51) - Assessment Centre (0.36–0.45) - Personality Test (0.31) - Unstructured Interview (0.20) - Reference Check (0.18) - Biodata (0.15) - [ ] **Aptitude Tests** measure *capacity*; **Personality Tests** measure *disposition*. - [ ] **STAR Method** — Situation, Task, Action, Result — used in Behavioral Interviews. - [ ] **Assessment Centre** = Multi-day, multi-exercise, multi-assessor simulation. **Gold standard for managerial selection.** - [ ] **Projective Tests** (Rorschach, TAT) = indirect personality assessment via ambiguous stimuli. - [ ] **Compensatory Rule** = weighted sum; **Conjunctive (Multiple Cut-Off)** = must pass all. - [ ] **Best Practice:** Combine cognitive + personality + structured interview + reference check. - [ ] **Validity Coefficient (r)** is the correlation between test score and job performance. - [ ] **Industrial application areas:** IT (aptitude tests), Aviation (stress + psychomotor), BPO (voice + EQ), Manufacturing (mechanical aptitude). ---

topics.title: Fair Employment Practices; Recruitment and Biographical Information
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT742 - INDUSTRIAL PSYCHOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fair Employment Practices; Recruitment and Biographical Information

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fair Employment Practices (FEP)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> **Fair Employment Practices** refer to a comprehensive set of organizational policies, legal statutes, and ethical standards designed to ensure that all individuals have an **equal opportunity** to secure employment, receive fair compensation, and progress in their careers *regardless* of race, color, religion, sex, national origin, age, disability, or genetic information.

In simpler terms, Fair Employment Practices are the **"rules of fair play"** in the workplace. They are designed so that hiring, firing, promotion, and all other employment decisions are made on the basis of **job-related criteria** (skills, experience, performance) and *not* on personal characteristics that have nothing to do with the job.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Two Pillars of FEP:**
> 1. **Equal Employment Opportunity (EEO)** – Giving everyone the same *chance* to compete.
> 2. **Affirmative Action (AA)** – Taking *active steps* to remove past barriers and improve representation of disadvantaged groups.

#### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Referee at the Sports Stadium"

Imagine a **100-meter sprint race** in a stadium. The referee blows the whistle, and every runner — whether tall, short, male, female, from any country — starts at the *same starting line*, runs on the *same track*, with the *same timing equipment*, and the first to cross the finish line honestly wins. The referee does not let anyone get a head start because of their surname, nor push anyone back because of their skin color.

**Fair Employment Practices is exactly this "referee."** It ensures:
- The job advertisement is the **starting gun** (open to all).
- The selection process is the **track** (standardized, transparent).
- The selection criteria are the **finish line** (job-related, measurable).
- No candidate is given a **head start or a handicap** based on irrelevant personal traits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Statistic/Standard Metric (Bolded):**
> In the United States, the primary law enforcing FEP is **Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964**, administered by the **Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC)**. Globally, the framework is mirrored by the **ILO Discrimination Convention (No. 111), 1958**.

---

### 1.2 Recruitment

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> **Recruitment** is the organizational process of **identifying, attracting, and screening** a pool of qualified candidates from which the organization can select the most appropriate person to fill a job vacancy. It is the *positive* step of *finding talent*.

#### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fishing Net"

Think of recruitment as **casting a fishing net into a vast ocean** of potential candidates. The organization decides:
- **Where to cast** (which job portals, campuses, agencies).
- **What bait to use** (the job description and employer brand).
- **How wide the net is** (local, national, or international).
- **The size of the mesh** (minimum qualifications, experience).

The goal is not to *catch one specific fish*, but to bring back a **good haul of varied, healthy fish** from which the chef (the *selector*) can later pick the best one for the recipe (the *job role*).

---

### 1.3 Biographical Information (Biodata)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> **Biographical Information (Biodata)** is a *systematic collection of an individual's past life experiences and factual history* — including education, work experience, personal achievements, interests, and demographic details — used in personnel selection to **predict future job performance and tenure**.

#### 1.3.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe of a Person"

If a job is a **dish to be cooked**, then a candidate's biodata is the **list of ingredients and their history**:
- The **flour** is their educational background.
- The **spices** are their skills and certifications.
- The **cooking experience** is their work history.
- The **past recipes they have made** are their previous achievements.

A good chef (recruiter) reads the recipe (biodata) to decide if this *combination of ingredients* can produce the dish (job performance) the restaurant (organization) needs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction in I/O Psychology:**
> - **Biodata** → Emphasizes *past, factual life events* (objective, historical).
> - **Personality Tests** → Measure *current traits* and dispositions.
> - **Interviews** → Assess *present behavior* and verbal responses.
> - **Cognitive Tests** → Measure *current mental abilities*.
> 
> Biodata is the **only one** of these that is purely **retrospective** and *non-invasive* in real time.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Legal Pillars of Fair Employment Practices

The architecture of FEP rests on three structural layers. A KTU student must remember the *hierarchy*:

1. **Constitutional / Foundational Layer** – Guarantees equality before the law (e.g., the 14th Amendment in the US; Articles 14, 15, 16 in the Indian Constitution).
2. **Statutory / Legislative Layer** – Specific laws criminalizing discrimination (e.g., Title VII, ADEA, ADA).
3. **Administrative / Enforcement Layer** – Bodies that implement the laws (e.g., EEOC; in India, the **National Commission for Scheduled Castes, National Commission for Women, etc.**).

### 2.2 Major Anti-Discrimination Laws (U.S. Framework — KTU Standard Reference)

| # | Law (Year) | Protected Class | Prohibited Action |
|---|------------|------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Title VII, Civil Rights Act (1964)** | Race, Color, Religion, Sex, National Origin | Discrimination in hiring, firing, promotion |
| 2 | **Equal Pay Act (1963)** | Sex | Unequal pay for substantially equal work |
| 3 | **Age Discrimination in Employment Act (ADEA, 1967)** | Age (40 and above) | Age-based discrimination |
| 4 | **Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA, 1990)** | Disability | Failure to provide *reasonable accommodation* |
| 5 | **Pregnancy Discrimination Act (1978)** | Pregnancy | Treating pregnancy as a disability |
| 6 | **Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act (GINA, 2008)** | Genetic Information | Discrimination based on DNA |
| 7 | **Civil Rights Act (1991)** | All above | Provided for *compensatory* and *punitive* damages |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Hot-Spot:** Students often confuse *Equal Pay Act* (focuses only on **sex-based pay**) with *Title VII* (covers all aspects of employment). Memorize the **distinct scope** of each Act.

### 2.3 Bona Fide Occupational Qualification (BFOQ)

> [!NOTE]
> A **Bona Fide Occupational Qualification (BFOQ)** is a *legally permitted exception* to anti-discrimination rules. It allows an employer to hire someone of a specific religion, sex, or national origin when that trait is *reasonably necessary* to the *normal operation* of the business.
> 
> **Examples (Legally Permitted):**
> - Hiring a female model to model women's lingerie.
> - Hiring a Catholic priest to lead a Catholic congregation.
> - Hiring a person of Chinese origin to authenticate Chinese antiques.
> 
> **Race is NEVER a BFOQ** under U.S. law. In India, Article 16(4) of the Constitution allows reservation in public employment for *Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes, and Other Backward Classes* — this is **not** a BFOQ but a *constitutional mandate*.

### 2.4 The Recruitment Process — Theoretical Breakdown

Recruitment is not a single act; it is a **pipeline of sub-processes**. The classical I/O Psychology model breaks it into **eight sequential logic steps**:

- **Step 1 — Job Analysis:** Identify the *KSAOs* (Knowledge, Skills, Abilities, Other characteristics) required.
- **Step 2 — Recruitment Planning:** Decide the *number*, *timing*, and *budget* of hires.
- **Step 3 — Sourcing Strategy:** Choose *internal* (promotions, transfers) vs *external* (advertisements, campus) sources.
- **Step 4 — Job Announcement:** Draft a *job description* (duties) and *job specification* (qualifications) using neutral, inclusive language.
- **Step 5 — Application Collection:** Accept résumés, application blanks, biodata forms.
- **Step 6 — Initial Screening:** Apply *knockout criteria* (e.g., minimum degree, work permit).
- **Step 7 — Pre-Employment Testing:** Biodata scoring, cognitive tests, personality inventories.
- **Step 8 — Final Selection & Job Offer:** Hire, onboard, orient.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:**
> - **Job Description** = What the person *does* (tasks, duties, responsibilities).
> - **Job Specification** = What the person *must be* (education, experience, skills, traits).
> 
> Many students interchange these in exams — this is a guaranteed 1–2 mark loss.

### 2.5 Internal vs. External Recruitment — Comparative Matrix

| Dimension | Internal Recruitment | External Recruitment |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Source** | Current employees (promotion, transfer, re-hire) | Job market (portals, campus, agencies) |
| **Cost** | **Lower** (no agency fees) | **Higher** (advertising, onboarding) |
| **Speed** | Faster (no notice period usually) | Slower |
| **Motivation** | Boosts morale of existing staff | May demotivate existing staff |
| **Fresh Talent** | Limited; "inbreeding" risk | **High**; new ideas, skills, diversity |
| **Training Need** | Less (already knows the org) | More (orientation required) |
| **Fairness Risk** | Can favor *favorites* and create bias | Can favor *unknown candidates* but with FEP-compliant screening |
| **Validity** | Higher for *predicting* cultural fit | Higher for *predicting* new skills |

### 2.6 Biographical Information — Theory and Scoring

Biographical data is one of the **oldest and most valid** selection methods in I/O Psychology. The core logic rests on the **"Consistency Principle":**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consistency Principle (Mumford & Stokes, 1992 — KTU Standard Reference)**
> *"Past behavior is the best predictor of future behavior."*
> A candidate's history of *achieving* (e.g., a 100% attendance record in school) is more likely to repeat in a future job that requires *high attendance* (e.g., a hospital nurse) than any single interview answer.

#### 2.6.1 Types of Biographical Information Items

Biodata items fall into three broad categories:

1. **Background & Demographic Items** (e.g., place of birth, parents' education).
2. **Experiential Items** (e.g., number of jobs held, years in a particular industry).
3. **Behavioral Tendency Items** (e.g., "In high school, I led at least 2 student clubs").

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Rule for FEP Compliance in Biodata:**
> Items that directly ask about *race, religion, sex, age, marital status, or disability* are **illegal** in most jurisdictions. They must be excluded unless **proven to be a BFOQ** (which is rare).

#### 2.6.2 Scoring Biodata — The Empirical Keying Method

The **empirical (or criterion-keying) method** is the standard scoring approach taught in KTU 2024 syllabus:

1. Collect biodata from a **sample of current employees** (both high and low performers).
2. Statistically correlate each biodata item with a **criterion measure** (e.g., supervisor rating, sales figures, tenure).
3. Assign **weights** to items based on their correlation strength.
4. Sum the weighted scores of an applicant's responses to obtain a **total biodata score**.
5. Use the total score to **rank and select** applicants.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for Module 3 numerical and conceptual questions.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Application / Use |
|---------|----------------|--------------------|
| **Selection Ratio** | $SR = \dfrac{\text{Number of Applicants Selected}}{\text{Number of Total Applicants}}$ | Lower SR → stricter selection → higher validity demand. KTU Standard: $SR < 0.15$ is "highly selective." |
| **Base Rate (BR)** | $BR = \dfrac{\text{Number of Successful Employees in Population}}{\text{Total Employees in Population}}$ | Used in **Taylor-Russell Tables** to predict gain in validity from a selection test. |
| **Utility of Selection (Brog-Cronbach-Davis formula)** | $\Delta U = N_{s} \cdot SD_{y} \cdot \overline{r} \cdot \Phi - \dfrac{N}{T}$ | Estimates *dollar gain* from using a selection procedure. |
| **Biodata Weight Score** | $S_{B} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{i} x_{i}$ where $w_{i}$ = item weight, $x_{i}$ = response code (0 or 1) | Total biodata score; threshold for shortlisting. |
| **Four-Fifths (4/5) Rule (EEOC)** | $\dfrac{\text{Selection rate of minority group}}{\text{Selection rate of majority group}} \geq 0.80$ | If below, adverse impact is *presumed*. |
| **Adverse Impact (AI)** | $AI = \dfrac{\text{Selection \% of protected class}}{\text{Selection \% of majority}}$ | $AI < 0.80$ = Adverse Impact exists. |
| **Standard Error of Difference (Sackett method)** | $SE_{D} = \sqrt{\dfrac{p_{1}(1-p_{1})}{N_{1}} + \dfrac{p_{2}(1-p_{2})}{N_{2}}}$ | Tests if difference between two selection rates is significant. |
| **Biserial Correlation** | $r_{b} = \dfrac{(\overline{X}_{p} - \overline{X}_{q})}{\sigma_{t}} \cdot \dfrac{pq}{y}$ | Used in empirical keying of biodata items. |
| **Point-Biserial Correlation** | $r_{pb} = \dfrac{M_{1} - M_{0}}{s_{n}} \cdot \sqrt{pq}$ | Used when one variable is dichotomous (e.g., pass/fail). |
| **Reliability (Cronbach's $\alpha$)** | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{k-1}\left(1 - \dfrac{\sum \sigma_{i}^{2}}{\sigma_{t}^{2}}\right)$ | Internal consistency reliability of a biodata form. |

### 2.8 Real-World Utility

- **In HR Departments:** Biodata + weighted application blanks are used by **TCS, Infosys, Amazon, and Walmart** for mass entry-level hiring where thousands of résumés must be screened quickly.
- **In Forensic & Security Hiring:** Biodata items are used to flag high-risk applicants (e.g., past termination for cause).
- **In Diversity Audits:** The 4/5 rule is used in *annual EEO-1 reports* filed with the EEOC to detect discrimination.
- **In Legal Defense:** If a selection system has documented *job-relatedness* (via biodata validation), it serves as a legal defense against *disparate impact* claims.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** The derivations below are written *exhaustively* for KTU 2024 board exam preparation. Each algebraic step is shown so a student can reproduce the answer on paper for full marks.

### 3.1 Derivation: The Adverse Impact (4/5ths) Rule Calculation

The **Uniform Guidelines on Employee Selection Procedures (UGESP, 1978)** uses the *4/5ths rule* (also called the *80% rule*) as a practical indicator of adverse impact. Let us derive the *decision rule* formally.

**Given:**
- $N_{M}$ = Number of majority-group applicants.
- $N_{m}$ = Number of minority-group applicants.
- $S_{M}$ = Number of majority-group applicants selected.
- $S_{m}$ = Number of minority-group applicants selected.

**Step 1: Compute the selection rate of each group.**

$$SR_{M} = \dfrac{S_{M}}{N_{M}}$$

$$SR_{m} = \dfrac{S_{m}}{N_{m}}$$

This is simply the ratio of "those selected" to "those who applied" within each group.

**Step 2: Compute the Adverse Impact Ratio (AIR).**

$$AIR = \dfrac{SR_{m}}{SR_{M}}$$

This normalizes the minority selection rate against the majority rate. An AIR of **1.00** means perfect parity; an AIR of **0.50** means the minority was selected at half the rate.

**Step 3: Apply the 4/5ths rule.**

$$AIR \geq 0.80 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{No Adverse Impact}$$

$$AIR < 0.80 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Adverse Impact is PRESUMED}$$

**Step 4: Test for statistical significance (Sackett's Standard Error test).**

The observed AIR may be due to chance. We test the difference between two proportions:

$$Z = \dfrac{SR_{M} - SR_{m}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_{1}(1-p_{1})}{N_{1}} + \dfrac{p_{2}(1-p_{2})}{N_{2}}}}$$

If $Z > \vert 1.96 \vert$ (i.e., $Z > 1.96$ or $Z < -1.96$ at the 0.05 level), the difference is **statistically significant**, and adverse impact is confirmed.

#### 3.1.1 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Question)

> **Problem:** An organization received **1,000** applications, of which **800** were from Group A and **200** from Group B. **400** from Group A and **60** from Group B were selected. Determine if adverse impact exists.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Selection rates.**

$$SR_{A} = \dfrac{400}{800} = 0.50 = 50\%$$

$$SR_{B} = \dfrac{60}{200} = 0.30 = 30\%$$

**Step 2: Adverse Impact Ratio.**

$$AIR = \dfrac{0.30}{0.50} = 0.60$$

**Step 3: Apply 4/5ths rule.**

Since $0.60 < 0.80$, **adverse impact is presumed**. The employer must justify the selection procedure as *job-related* and *consistent with business necessity*.

**Step 4: Significance test.**

$$Z = \dfrac{0.50 - 0.30}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.50 \times 0.50}{800} + \dfrac{0.30 \times 0.70}{200}}}$$

$$Z = \dfrac{0.20}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.25}{800} + \dfrac{0.21}{200}}}$$

$$Z = \dfrac{0.20}{\sqrt{0.0003125 + 0.00105}}$$

$$Z = \dfrac{0.20}{\sqrt{0.0013625}}$$

$$Z = \dfrac{0.20}{0.0369} \approx 5.42$$

Since $Z = 5.42 > 1.96$, the difference is **statistically significant at $p < 0.05$**.

**Conclusion:** Adverse impact is *both* legally presumed (by 4/5ths rule) *and* statistically significant. The employer faces a **disparate impact** claim.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Biodata Weighted Score

**Given:** A biodata form has $n$ items, each weighted $w_{i}$ and scored $x_{i} \in \{0, 1\}$ (0 = "no", 1 = "yes").

**Step 1: Write the score of one applicant.**

$$S_{B}^{(1)} = w_{1}x_{1} + w_{2}x_{2} + w_{3}x_{3} + \ldots + w_{n}x_{n}$$

**Step 2: Express as a summation.**

$$S_{B}^{(1)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{i} x_{i}$$

**Step 3: For a batch of $N$ applicants, the matrix form is useful.**

$$\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{X} \cdot \mathbf{w}$$

where $\mathbf{X}$ is the $N \times n$ response matrix, $\mathbf{w}$ is the $n \times 1$ weight vector, and $\mathbf{S}$ is the $N \times 1$ vector of total scores.

**Step 4: Deriving item weights via empirical keying (biserial correlation).**

For a dichotomously scored item $x_{i} \in \{0, 1\}$ and a continuous criterion $Y$ (e.g., job performance rating):

$$r_{b_{i}} = \dfrac{\overline{Y}_{1} - \overline{Y}_{0}}{\sigma_{Y}} \cdot \dfrac{p_{i}q_{i}}{\varphi(z_{i})}$$

where:
- $\overline{Y}_{1}$ = mean criterion score of those answering "yes" on item $i$.
- $\overline{Y}_{0}$ = mean criterion score of those answering "no" on item $i$.
- $p_{i}$ = proportion answering "yes"; $q_{i} = 1 - p_{i}$.
- $\varphi(z_{i})$ = height of the standard normal curve at the point where $p_{i}$ proportion of the area lies below.

**Step 5: Set the weight equal to the biserial correlation.**

$$w_{i} = r_{b_{i}}$$

This is the *empirical keying* approach — each item's weight is *proportional* to its *empirical relationship* with the criterion.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Selection Methods

| Method | Validity (typically) | Adverse Impact Risk | Cost | Applicant Reaction | FEP Compliance |
|--------|----------------------|---------------------|------|---------------------|----------------|
| **Cognitive Ability Test** | High ($r \approx 0.50$–$0.60$) | **High** (cultural subgroup differences) | Moderate | Mixed | Needs *careful* validation |
| **Biodata / WAB** | Moderate–High ($r \approx 0.30$–$0.50$) | Low–Moderate | Low | Favorable | **High** (if empirically keyed) |
| **Structured Interview** | Moderate ($r \approx 0.40$) | Low | High | Favorable | High |
| **Unstructured Interview** | **Low** ($r \approx 0.20$) | **High** | High | Favorable | Low |
| **Personality Test** | Low–Moderate ($r \approx 0.20$) | Low | Low | Mixed | High |
| **Work Sample / Job Tryout** | **Very High** ($r \approx 0.60$) | **Very Low** | Very High | Favorable | Very High |
| **Reference Check** | Low ($r \approx 0.20$) | Low | Moderate | Favorable | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Notice the *inverse* relationship between *validity* and *adverse impact risk* in cognitive tests. This is the central tension in FEP — the **validity–diversity tradeoff** that the 1991 Civil Rights Act tried to balance via the "business necessity" defense.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1 | 3 Marks]**
> **Q: Define Fair Employment Practices. List any three major U.S. laws that support FEP.**

**Model Answer:**

Fair Employment Practices (FEP) refers to the set of policies, laws, and organizational practices that ensure individuals are given **equal opportunity** in all aspects of employment — hiring, promotion, compensation, training, and termination — based on job-related criteria rather than personal characteristics such as race, color, religion, sex, national origin, age, or disability.

**Three major U.S. laws supporting FEP:**
1. **Title VII of the Civil Rights Act (1964)** — prohibits discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, and national origin.
2. **Age Discrimination in Employment Act (ADEA, 1967)** — protects employees aged 40 and above from age-based discrimination.
3. **Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA, 1990)** — prohibits discrimination against qualified individuals with disabilities and requires *reasonable accommodation*.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2 | 3 Marks]**
> **Q: What is Biographical Information? State the Consistency Principle on which biodata is based.**

**Model Answer:**

**Biographical Information (Biodata)** is a collection of factual data about an applicant's past life events, including educational history, work experiences, achievements, interests, and demographic background, used in personnel selection to predict future job success.

**Consistency Principle:** The principle underlying biodata states that *"past behavior is the best predictor of future behavior."* This means that an individual's demonstrated patterns in past life situations (such as academic consistency, leadership history, or job stability) are likely to recur in similar future job situations. Hence, scoring an applicant on their *past factual life events* provides a valid, retrospective indicator of *future job performance* and *tenure*.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A | 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** Explain the concept of **Adverse Impact** in selection. How is it measured using the **4/5ths rule**? Illustrate with a numerical example. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss the **major steps in the recruitment process**. Compare **internal and external recruitment** with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

##### **Model Solution (a):**

**Definition of Adverse Impact:**
Adverse Impact refers to a substantially *disproportionate rate of selection* in employment decisions that works to the disadvantage of a protected class (e.g., women, minorities, older workers), even when the employer's *intent* is not to discriminate. It is a form of *disparate impact discrimination*.

**The 4/5ths Rule (EEOC / UGESP, 1978):**
The rule is operationalized as follows:
1. Compute the *selection rate* of the protected group: $SR_{\text{minority}} = \dfrac{\text{Selected}_{\text{minority}}}{\text{Applied}_{\text{minority}}}$
2. Compute the *selection rate* of the majority group: $SR_{\text{majority}} = \dfrac{\text{Selected}_{\text{majority}}}{\text{Applied}_{\text{majority}}}$
3. Form the ratio: $AIR = \dfrac{SR_{\text{minority}}}{SR_{\text{majority}}}$
4. Apply the rule:
   - If $AIR \geq 0.80$ → No adverse impact.
   - If $AIR < 0.80$ → Adverse impact is **presumed**; the employer must prove the selection procedure is *job-related* and *consistent with business necessity*.

**Numerical Example:**

A company received **500** applications: **400** from Group A and **100** from Group B. **200** from Group A and **30** from Group B were selected.

$$SR_{A} = \dfrac{200}{400} = 0.50$$

$$SR_{B} = \dfrac{30}{100} = 0.30$$

$$AIR = \dfrac{0.30}{0.50} = 0.60$$

Since $0.60 < 0.80$, **adverse impact exists**. The employer must either modify the selection procedure or justify it as a *business necessity*.

##### **Model Solution (b):**

**Major Steps in the Recruitment Process:**

1. **Job Analysis:** Identify the KSAOs (Knowledge, Skills, Abilities, Other characteristics) required.
2. **Recruitment Planning:** Decide the *number of hires*, *timing*, and *budget*.
3. **Sourcing Strategy:** Choose *internal* (promotion, transfer) or *external* (advertisements, campus) sources.
4. **Job Announcement:** Draft a *job description* and *job specification* in neutral, inclusive language.
5. **Application Collection:** Accept résumés, application blanks, and biodata forms.
6. **Initial Screening:** Apply *knockout criteria* (e.g., minimum degree, work permit).
7. **Pre-Employment Testing:** Biodata scoring, cognitive tests, personality inventories.
8. **Final Selection & Job Offer:** Hire, onboard, and orient the new employee.

**Internal vs External Recruitment — Comparison:**

| Aspect | Internal Recruitment | External Recruitment |
|--------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Examples** | Promotion, transfer, re-hire of ex-employees | Campus placement, job portals, headhunters |
| **Cost** | Low (no agency fees) | High (advertising, onboarding) |
| **Speed** | Fast | Slow |
| **Fresh Talent** | Limited | **High** |
| **Morale** | Boosts existing staff | May cause *demotivation* if overused |
| **Validity** | High for cultural fit | High for *new skills* |
| **Risk** | Inbreeding, favoritism | Cultural mismatch, longer training |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B (a) & (b):**
> - Students often **forget the statistical significance step** (Sackett's $Z$ test) after applying the 4/5ths rule. The rule alone is only a *rule of thumb*; a board answer that does not mention the *significance test* will lose 1 mark.
> - In Part (b), students confuse **"job description" with "job specification."** Use the *exact KTU terms*: description = duties; specification = qualifications.
> - Avoid writing *only* the advantages of internal recruitment without addressing its **disadvantages** (inbreeding, limited diversity). The examiner expects a *balanced* comparison.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question B | 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** Define **Recruitment**. Explain the **sources of recruitment** in detail. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** What is **Biographical Information**? Explain the **empirical keying method** of scoring biodata with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

##### **Model Solution (a):**

**Definition of Recruitment:**
Recruitment is the organizational process of **identifying, attracting, and screening** a pool of qualified candidates from which the most suitable individual can be selected to fill a job vacancy. It is a *positive* activity aimed at *building a talent pipeline*.

**Sources of Recruitment:**

Recruitment sources are divided into **two main categories**:

**(i) Internal Sources:**
- **Promotions:** Moving an existing employee to a higher-level role.
- **Transfers:** Shifting an employee to a different role at the *same level* in a different location or department.
- **Re-employment / Recall:** Rehiring former employees who left in good standing.
- **Employee Referrals:** Existing employees recommend candidates from their network.
- **Job Posting (Internal Job Board):** Vacancies are first advertised within the organization.

**(ii) External Sources:**
- **Direct Walk-in / Campus Recruitment:** Company visits colleges to hire fresh graduates.
- **Advertisement (Print, Digital, Job Portals):** Naukri, LinkedIn, Indeed, etc.
- **Employment Exchanges (Government):** Public-sector hiring in India.
- **Recruitment Agencies / Headhunters:** Third-party firms for specialized roles.
- **E-Recruitment / Social Media Recruiting:** LinkedIn, Twitter, Facebook job ads.
- **Consultants and Placement Firms:** Used for executive-level hiring.
- **Word of Mouth / Public Notices:** Notice boards, public announcements.

##### **Model Solution (b):**

**Definition of Biographical Information (Biodata):**
Biographical Information is a *systematic compilation* of an applicant's past life events, experiences, achievements, education, and personal history, used in selection to *predict* future job performance based on the *consistency principle* — past behavior predicts future behavior.

**Empirical (Criterion) Keying Method:**

The empirical keying method is a *statistical* approach to developing and scoring biodata forms. It involves the following steps:

**Step 1 — Identify the Criterion:** Choose a measure of job success (e.g., supervisor's performance rating, sales figures, tenure).

**Step 2 — Collect Biodata:** Administer a *trial* biodata form to a sample of *current* employees (both high and low performers).

**Step 3 — Score Responses:** Code each item as 0 (e.g., "no") or 1 (e.g., "yes").

**Step 4 — Correlate with Criterion:** For each item, compute its *biserial correlation* ($r_b$) with the criterion.

**Step 5 — Assign Weights:** Items with *statistically significant* positive correlations receive their $r_b$ as a weight; items with *no* significant correlation are dropped.

**Step 6 — Final Form:** The surviving weighted items form the *final biodata inventory*. The applicant's total score is:

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{i} x_{i}$$

**Numerical Example:**
Suppose a retail company wants to hire sales staff. It gives 20 trial biodata items to 200 current employees. The criterion is *annual sales in rupees*. The biserial correlation of one item — *"I won at least 2 academic prizes in school"* — is found to be $r_b = +0.42$ ($p < 0.05$). This item is *retained* with weight $w = 0.42$. Another item — *"I have a pet dog"* — has $r_b = -0.05$ (n.s.) and is *dropped*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> - In Part (a), students often list sources *without classifying* them as *internal vs external*. Examiners explicitly look for the **two-category structure**. A 1-mark deduction occurs if the classification is missing.
> - In Part (b), students write the **logical keying method** (where experts assign weights) by mistake instead of **empirical keying** (where weights come from correlations). The question *explicitly* asks for empirical keying — wrong method = 3-mark penalty.
> - **Do not confuse** *biodata* with *résumé*. A biodata is a *standardized, weighted form*; a résumé is a *self-authored* document.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3**

- **Fair Employment Practices (FEP):** Equal opportunity + Affirmative Action. Enforced by **Title VII, ADEA, ADA, EPA, GINA** in the U.S.
- **EEO vs. AA:** EEO = *passive* (no barriers); AA = *active* (correct past discrimination).
- **4/5ths Rule:** AIR $< 0.80$ → Adverse Impact presumed. Always pair with a **Sackett significance test** for full credit.
- **BFOQ:** A *narrow* legal exception. **Race is never a BFOQ.** Religion, sex, national origin *can* be, in very limited cases.
- **Recruitment ≠ Selection:** Recruitment is *attracting* candidates; Selection is *choosing* from them. This distinction is asked in almost every KTU exam.
- **Job Description vs. Job Specification:** Description = *what they do*; Specification = *what they must have*.
- **Biodata:** Based on the **Consistency Principle** — *"past predicts future."*
- **Empirical Keying:** Weights come from *statistical correlations*, not from expert judgment.
- **Biserial Correlation** is the *correct* statistic for scoring dichotomous biodata items; **point-biserial** is used when the criterion is *truly* dichotomous (e.g., retained/not retained).
- **Selection Ratio:** $SR < 0.15$ = highly selective → use only *high-validity* tools.
- **FEP-Compliant Biodata:** Never ask directly about race, religion, sex, age, or disability unless *provably* a BFOQ.
- **KTU Hot-Spot Statutes:** Title VII (1964), EPA (1963), ADEA (1967), PDA (1978), ADA (1990), GINA (2008), CRA (1991) — **memorize the year-order** for chronology questions.
- **Validity–Diversity Tradeoff:** High-validity tools (e.g., cognitive tests) often have **high adverse impact**; lower-validity tools (e.g., biodata, structured interviews) often have **low adverse impact** — a *practical* tension that I/O psychologists must navigate.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Industrial and Organizational (I-O) Psychology**, an **interview** is formally defined as a *purposeful, structured (or semi-structured) dyadic or multi-person verbal exchange* conducted between a representative(s) of an organization and a candidate (or group of candidates), designed primarily to **assess suitability, gather predictive information, and facilitate selection, placement, promotion, or counseling decisions** concerning that individual.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PESFT742)** framework, the interview is positioned as one of the most widely used **employee selection techniques**, occupying the position of a *socio-psychological assessment tool* that bridges the candidate's self-disclosed information and the organization's person-job fit requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The interview is studied not just as a "questioning event," but as a **psychological measurement instrument** — meaning it has *validity, reliability, and susceptibility to bias*, much like any other psychometric tool.

The classical operational definition attributed to **E. Sidney & M. Brown (1961)** describes an interview as a *"conversation with a purpose."* KTU examiners expect students to recognize this foundational phrasing because it appears in module questions repeatedly.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are buying a used car. You don't just glance at the paint job — you **talk to the seller**, ask about mileage, service history, accident records, and how the car *feels* on a test drive. The conversation itself (not just a spec sheet) reveals hidden information.

That is exactly what an **industrial interview** is:

| Everyday Analogy | Industrial Psychology Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Buyer's questions to seller | Interviewer's questions to candidate |
| Seller's body language & honesty | Candidate's non-verbal cues & responses |
| Test drive of the car | Work-sample / scenario-based questioning |
| Buyer's gut feeling | Interviewer's **clinical/impressionistic judgment** |
| Mechanic's inspection report | Structured rating scales & scorecards |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The interview is the *only* selection technique where the candidate and the assessor *interact in real-time*. This makes it rich in information but also highly vulnerable to **human error, stereotypes, and the "halo/horns effect"** — a recurring KTU long-answer theme.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Interview Assessment

When an interview is treated as a psychometric instrument, the following standardized measurement concepts apply (these are KTU exam-relevant terms):

- **Predictive Validity** — the degree to which interview scores actually forecast future job performance. Typical reported values: **r ≈ 0.20 to 0.51** (meta-analytic estimate of structured interviews).
- **Reliability (Inter-rater)** — agreement between two interviewers scoring the same candidate. Acceptable benchmark: **r ≥ 0.70**.
- **Standard Error of Measurement (SEM)** — captured via the formula:
  
  $$SEM = SD \cdot \sqrt{1 - r_{xx}}$$
  
  where $SD$ is the standard deviation of interview scores and $r_{xx}$ is the reliability coefficient. The vertical bar / pipe restriction is honored by using $\sqrt{}$ notation.

- **Effect Size (Cohen's d)** — used to compare structured vs. unstructured interview performance, typically **d ≈ 0.51** favoring structured formats (McDaniel et al., 1994 meta-analysis cited in I-O literature).

> [!TIP]
> **Why these metrics matter in KTU answers:** Examiners award marks when students demonstrate that an interview is *not* just a chat — it is a **measurable** decision-making tool with quantifiable properties.
